content.assessment: Delaying selection to age 14 removes two of three early sorting points, which — per the robust big-fish-little-pond literature — should reduce the number of junctures at which equally able students are re-sorted into reference groups that could depress academic self-concept. Later, single-point tracking also aligns with goal-orientation evidence suggesting that fewer high-stakes sorting events may reduce chronic performance-orientation and fear-of-failure framing in favor of longer exposure to mastery-oriented, heterogeneous classrooms, though developmental timing effects are moderate rather than strong evidence. Delayed tracking is also more consistent with self-determination theory's emphasis on non-controlling environments, since students face fewer externally imposed high-stakes sorting decisions before age 14. However, the transition process itself (as in the Polish case) could introduce short-term cognitive load and uncertainty for displaced cohorts, and the psychological benefits of delayed selection depend on implementation quality, not guaranteed by structural change alone.
opportunities: A single later selection point could be designed with mastery-oriented, low-stakes framing to further offset performance-orientation risks
Reduced early sorting may lower stereotype-threat-adjacent labeling exposure for young children, though stereotype-threat evidence itself is contested and should not be overstated
strengths: Removing two early selection points reduces the frequency of reference-group re-sorting events linked to self-concept costs in the strong big-fish-little-pond evidence
Later single-entry-point tracking is more consistent with mastery-oriented goal structures than repeated early sorting, per moderate goal-orientation evidence
Fewer imposed sorting decisions before age 14 aligns with self-determination theory's emphasis on autonomy-supportive rather than controlling structures
threats: Implementation disruption (e.g., double-cohort transition problems) could itself generate acute stress, uncertainty, and motivational disruption for affected students independent of the structural benefits
Political conflict and resistance from maintainers could produce inconsistent implementation, undermining the uniform psychological benefits the mechanism assumes
weaknesses: Psychological benefits of delayed tracking are context-dependent and the cited reform evidence is drawn from a different national system, limiting direct transfer certainty
Any single-point selection at 14 still carries big-fish-little-pond and labeling risks; delay does not eliminate reference-group effects, only reduces their frequency
